title: Double Outdoor Chaise Lounger
summary: A two-person reclining lounger reusing the same hinge-prop-and-stop-block backrest mechanism as this catalog's single chaise, widened to a shared deck for two — not just the same design copied.
description:
The deck is one wide 2x4 frame decked in 1x4 boards, sized for two people to lounge side by side rather than two separate chairs pushed together. Build the frame first with the longer screws and exterior glue, attach the legs keeping the back rest bay clear, then add 2x2 cleats inside that bay flush to the bottom — these are what the hinged backrest mechanism will attach to later.

**The reclining mechanism is the same hinge-prop-stop-block system as this catalog's single chaise lounge**, just built at double width to support two backrests side by side: 2x2 pieces hinged to 2x4 prop-up spacers let the whole backrest kick up and rest against stop blocks at whatever angle you've positioned them. Build the two back pieces with their own 1x4 slats attached to 2x2 backrest supports, then attach the prop-ups before hinging the entire assembly to the main frame.

**Position the stop blocks only after test-fitting the backrest closed** — the plan is explicit that you need to confirm the back can fully close with the blocks in place before committing to their final position. A generous supply of roughly 16"-long 1x4 offcuts is left over from this build, which is exactly the material this catalog's Adirondack side tables and footstool are sized around, if you want a matching set.

materials.name: Exterior penetrating wood stain or paint

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body:
Every cut here is square. From the 2x4: two frame sides at 76-1/2", four front, back and support pieces at 44-1/2", six legs at 11-1/4" and three prop-up spacers at 10". From the 2x2: four backrest supports at 43-1/2", two backrest cleats at 27" and four stop blocks at 3". From the 1x4: two long deck pieces at 79-1/2", eleven centre deck pieces at 49-1/2" and eleven back slats at 30".

The 1x4 lands exactly on the thirteen boards listed if you give the two 79-1/2" pieces their own boards and then cut one 49-1/2" centre piece plus one 30" slat from each of the remaining eleven. That layout is also where the roughly 16-1/2" offcuts the description mentions come from — 96" less 49-1/2" less 30" — so cut in that order and you will have them.

steps.title: Build the frame
steps.body:
Lay the two 76-1/2" 2x4 sides on edge and parallel, and set the four 44-1/2" pieces between them — one at the head, one at the foot and the other two spaced along the run as intermediate supports, because a deck this long will flex without them. Drive two of the longer deck screws per joint through the side into the end grain, with exterior wood glue on every mating face.

The finished frame is 76-1/2" long and 47-1/2" wide over the outside faces. That is your check: the 79-1/2" long deck pieces should overhang 1-1/2" at each end and the 49-1/2" centre pieces 1" at each side. Measure both diagonals and rack the frame until they match before the glue grabs.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body:
Fasten the six 11-1/4" legs inside the frame at the corners and at the intermediate supports, with the longer deck screws driven through two faces of the frame into each leg so no leg can pivot on a single line of fasteners.

Keep the backrest bay — the section of frame the hinged back drops into — completely clear of legs. A leg inside that bay stops the backrest closing flat, and you will not discover it until the back is already hinged on. Stand the frame on a flat surface and confirm it does not rock before going further.

steps.title: Add the backrest cleats
steps.body:
Run a 27" 2x2 cleat along the inside face of each frame side within the backrest bay, flush with the bottom edge of the 2x4. Flush to the bottom is what leaves the full depth of the frame above the cleat for the backrest to sit down into, so it closes level with the deck instead of standing proud.

Fasten with the longer screws and exterior glue, putting screws along the whole length rather than just at the ends — these cleats carry the hinges and every reclining load that goes through them.

steps.title: Deck the frame
steps.body:
Re-check the frame for square, then glue and screw the two 79-1/2" long deck pieces along the outer edges with the shorter screws, two at each crossing, letting them overhang 1-1/2" at each end.

Fill in with the eleven 49-1/2" centre pieces, spacing them evenly with a small gap between boards so rain drains rather than sits on the deck. Cut a scrap spacer and use it at every gap instead of measuring each one — gaps set by eye drift, and all the drift lands on the last board. Leave the backrest bay open; no decking goes over it.

steps.title: Build the back pieces
steps.body:
Lay the four 43-1/2" 2x2 backrest supports out as the backrest's crossbars and run the eleven 30" 1x4 slats over them, fastening each slat to each support with two of the shorter screws. Eleven slats at 3-1/2" is 38-1/2" of board across a 43-1/2" support, so ten even gaps of 1/2" bring the slat field out to exactly 43-1/2" — cut a 1/2" spacer and use it every time.

One thing to settle before you cut or fasten: the description calls for two side-by-side backrests, but those 43-1/2" supports and this slat arithmetic describe a single full-width backrest spanning the 44-1/2" bay. Check the photo and build to whichever it actually shows. Then fasten the 10" prop-up spacers to the backrest supports with the longer screws and hinge the prop-up piece to them.

steps.title: Attach the prop-ups to the backs
steps.body: Attach the prop-up assemblies to the back pieces with the shorter screws.

steps.title: Hinge the backrests to the frame
steps.body:
Hinge the backrest assembly to the frame along the cleats you set in the backrest bay, with the hinge knuckles on the top face so the back swings up and lies back down flush with the deck. Space the hinges evenly across the width and drive every screw into solid 2x2, never into the end grain of a slat.

Swing the backrest through its full travel before the last hinge screw goes in. A hinge line that is even slightly out of parallel will bind, and it is far easier to move one hinge now than to re-drill a line of them later.

steps.title: Position the stop blocks
steps.body:
Close the backrest fully with the prop-ups in place and confirm it actually lands flat — the plan is explicit that this check comes before the stop blocks are fixed, because a block in the wrong place stops the back closing at all.

Then set the four 3" 2x2 stop blocks inside the frame where the prop-up lands at each reclining position you want, and fasten with the longer screws. Test every position with real weight on the back before you trust it; a stop block that skips under load is the one failure that dumps somebody on the deck.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces and apply exterior stain or paint.
materials: Exterior penetrating wood stain or paint

images.alt: Double outdoor chaise lounger with reclining backrests
